focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

Mars had a way of paving over old sins with useful things. The back stair behind the Chou arcade was gone, replaced by a freight elevator painted with black-and-yellow hazard stripes. The vent cavity behind the pawn broker on Ninth had been filled with pink sealant that bulged through the grate like cheap sausage. The room above the machine-parts wholesaler, where Spike had once slept through an afternoon with a pistol under his hand and dried blood cracking at his ankle, had become a tutoring center. Paper planets hung in the window. One had peeled loose and stuck to the glass from static, Saturn turned on its side over Tharsis.

They hadn't gone near Mars that night. Daylight was a different bargain.

He checked anyway.

He checked the old cigarette machine with the false back. The machine was gone and the wall behind it had been tiled over in white squares already going yellow at the grout. He checked the drain trench behind a shuttered bathhouse where message capsules used to sit in the third slot down. The whole block had been torn open for a utility line. Rebar stuck out of a trench full of pale dust and plastic cups. He crossed into the older freight alleys and found one steel door welded shut, another with three fresh locks on it and a remnant gang tag sprayed across the frame in silver loops. It wasn't Red Dragon. It was something newer, with the same cheap paint and the same threat.

The wind came down the loading lanes with red grit in it, hissing over the concrete. By noon the sun had thinned to a dirty coin behind the settlement haze, and the yards were all sound and pressure: forklifts whining in reverse, pallet jacks clattering over seams in the slab, the low thump of cargo pods settling into magnetic cradles. A woman in insulated coveralls s...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...into place and tested it once. The cabinet door closed smooth as if it had never stuck.

It was a small, stupid success. He did not look happier for it.

He wiped his hand on the rag and looked at Ed. "Set a proximity alarm on the ship too. If it powers up, if a hatch opens, if somebody so much as breathes hard near it, wake all of us."

Ed's head came farther off the couch. "All of all?"

"All of all."

"Alarm gremlin deployed," Ed said, and the console windows shuffled. The passive-watch strip widened. A fresh local note field bloomed beside the old bounty file, tagged with Miriam's name and Ganymede in Ed's uneven capitalization.

A dry bureaucratic chirp came from the console. One of the archive panes refreshed, the old dormant line blinking once before harder letters stamped themselves across the screen.

"Oops," Ed said, interested rather than alarmed. "Sleepy bounty woke up."

`WARRANT STATUS: REACTIVATED.`

Faye took the noodles to the table and sat. The box warmed her hands through the cardboard. Across from her, Hosuke Takara stared from the console in bad old resolution. Beside him sat Miriam's new note window, the bright reactivated warrant line, and the crawl of the passive watch. By the window the receiver reflected a thin strip of Mars light. Under the sink, Jet sorted the last two screws and put them away as if the order of tiny metal objects might still count for something.

No one said what they had actually found, not all of it.

The air recycler hummed. The hull ticked as the temperature dropped outside. Somewhere down the corridor, water knocked once in the pipes and stopped. Faye opened the noodle box, lifted the chopsticks, pressed her knuckles flat to the table until the tendons stood out, and ate while the ship listened for the stranger outside.
